questions.text: Locus of a gene on a chromosome means its:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The locus of a gene on a chromosome refers to its specific location or position on the chromosome. It is the precise point on the chromosome where the gene is situated, and it is usually described by its chromosomal coordinates (e.g., 3q21, meaning the gene is located on the long arm of chromosome 3, at position 21).
The function of a gene refers to its role or purpose in the cell, not its location on the chromosome.
The start point of a gene refers to the beginning of the gene's coding sequence, not its position on the chromosome.
The end point of a gene refers to the end of the gene's coding sequence, not its position on the chromosome.

questions.explanation: The locus of a gene on a chromosome refers to its specific location or position on the chromosome. It is the precise point on the chromosome where the gene is situated, and it is usually described by its chromosomal coordinates (e.g., 3q21, meaning the gene is located on the long arm of chromosome 3, at position 21).

questions.text: The concept that the gene pool of an idealised, non-evolving population remains constant over generations forms the basis of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: ❌ Mendel’s Principle of Segregation → Describes how alleles separate during gamete formation—not about gene pool stability.
❌ Mendel’s Principle of Independent Assortment → Explains how different genes independently segregate—not population genetics.
❌ Darwin’s Theory of Natural Selection → Describes how advantageous traits increase—not about a constant gene pool.

options.text: Darwin’s Theory of Survival of the Fittest.
options.explanation: ❌ Darwin’s Theory of Survival of the Fittest → A summary of natural selection, not a model for genetic equilibrium.
✅ The Principle of Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium → Correct. It states that allele and genotype frequencies remain constant in a non-evolving population under certain conditions.

questions.explanation: The concept that the gene pool of an idealized, non-evolving population remains constant over generations is known as the Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium. It forms the basis of population genetics and helps understand how genetic variation is maintained or changes within a population over time. The Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium describes the conditions under which the frequencies of alleles and genotypes in a population will remain stable from generation to generation in the absence of evolutionary forces such as mutation, genetic drift, migration, selection, and non-random mating.

questions.text: If, on average, 46% of the loci in a species' gene pool are heterozygous, then the average homozygosity of the species should be:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: There is not enough information to say.

questions.explanation: This question is related to the concept of genetic variability within a species. In genetics, a locus refers to a specific position on a chromosome where a gene is located. The gene pool of a species represents all the genetic material or variations present in that species. Heterozygosity refers to the condition where an organism has two different alleles (alternative forms of a gene) at a specific locus. In other words, heterozygous individuals have inherited different versions of a gene from each parent. Homozygosity, on the other hand, refers to the condition where an organism has two identical alleles at a specific locus. Homozygous individuals have inherited the same version of a gene from both parents. The question states that, on average, 46% of the loci in a species' gene pool are heterozygous. Since the gene pool represents all the genetic variations in the species, this means that 46% of the loci have individuals that inherited different versions of the gene at those positions. To determine the average homozygosity of the species, we need to subtract the heterozygous loci percentage from 100%. This is because the remaining loci that are not heterozygous would be homozygous. So, the average homozygosity of the species would be: 100% - 46% = 54% Therefore, the correct answer is C. 54%.

questions.text: Genes P, Q, R, and S occur on the same chromosome.Investigation of a large population produced the following cross-over values between pairs of genes.P and R 34% P and Q 59% R and S 12% S and Q 37% Which of the following sequences represents the sequence of genes on the chromosome?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This arrangement suggests P and R are adjacent, which contradicts the high crossover value of 59% between P and Q. Therefore, this option is unlikely to be correct.
This sequence fits the crossover values well, as it places S next to R (12% crossover), which is the lowest value, and aligns with the higher crossover value between P and Q (59%).
This arrangement places Q at the beginning, which does not support the high crossover value between P and Q (59%). Thus, this option is incorrect.
This sequence places R before S, which is inconsistent with the low crossover value of 12% between R and S. Therefore, this option is also incorrect.
This arrangement suggests S is adjacent to P, conflicting with the high crossover value of 59% between P and Q. Hence, this option is not correct.

questions.explanation: The correct sequence of genes on the chromosome is PSRQ. This arrangement makes sense given the crossover values provided:The low crossover value of 12% between R and S indicates they are closely linked, which is consistent with having them next to each other in this sequence.The higher crossover value of 59% between P and Q suggests they are further apart on the chromosome, which is also supported in this arrangement.The other options (PRSQ, QSPR, RQSP, SPRQ) do not appropriately reflect the crossover data, leading to contradictions with the provided percentages.

questions.text: After a mutation at a genetic locus, the character of an organism changes due to a change in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: After a mutation at a genetic locus, the character of an organism changes due to a change in protein structure. Mutations can occur in the DNA of an organism, which in turn leads to a mutation in the RNA synthetic pattern, which causes a mutation in protein structure.
DNA replication is the process of copying DNA. It is not directly involved in changing the character of an organism.
The protein synthesis pattern is the way in which proteins are synthesised from RNA. It is not directly involved in changing the character of an organism.
RNA transcription pattern is the way in which RNA is transcribed from DNA. It is not directly involved in changing the character of an organism.

questions.explanation: After a mutation at a genetic locus, the character of an organism changes due to a change in protein structure. Mutations can occur in the DNA of an organism, which can lead to changes in the RNA that is transcribed from that DNA. This, in turn, can lead to changes in the proteins that are synthesised from that RNA. If the mutation occurs in a protein-coding region of the DNA, it can lead to a change in the amino acid sequence of the protein. This change in amino acid sequence can lead to a change in the protein's structure and function.The other options are incorrect because:DNA replication is the process of copying DNA. It is not directly involved in changing the character of an organism.The protein synthesis pattern is the way in which proteins are synthesised from RNA. It is not directly involved in changing the character of an organism.RNA transcription pattern is the way in which RNA is transcribed from DNA. It is not directly involved in changing the character of an organism.

questions.text: The results of a linkage analysis of genes A, B, C, and D have just come in from the lab. In what order are the alleles found on the chromosome?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: ABCD is not the correct order of the genes based on the linkage analysis results.
DCAB is the correct order of genes as determined by the linkage analysis.
CDAB is not the correct order of the genes. It does not match the linkage analysis results.
ACDB is not the correct order of the genes. The linkage analysis results indicate otherwise.

questions.explanation: The correct order of genes on the chromosome is DCAB, as determined by the linkage analysis. Linkage analysis helps in mapping the distance and order of genes on a chromosome based on the frequency of recombination. The analysis results indicate that DCAB is the correct sequence. The other options (ABCD, CDAB, ACDB) do not match the order derived from the linkage analysis and are therefore incorrect.

questions.text: Which of the following would not be a feature seen in a patient with the following karyotype?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Many loops on fingertips are a dermatoglyphic feature often associated with Down's syndrome. Therefore, a patient with the given karyotype may exhibit this characteristic.
Congenital heart disease is a common complication in individuals with Down's syndrome, such as atrioventricular septal defects. Hence, this is not the feature we are looking for.
A large and wrinkled tongue is a typical feature observed in individuals with Down's syndrome. Therefore, it is not the correct answer.
Mucus clogging of airways is characteristic of cystic fibrosis, not Down's syndrome. Therefore, someone with the given karyotype would not exhibit this feature. This is the correct option.

questions.explanation: The given karyotype indicates Down's syndrome, which is characterized by trisomy 21. Common features of Down's syndrome include dermatoglyphic patterns like loops on fingertips, congenital heart defects, and a large, furrowed tongue. However, mucus clogging of airways is specific to cystic fibrosis, not Down's syndrome, making it the feature that would not be seen in a patient with this karyotype.

questions.text: Position of a gene on the chromosome is called its:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: a) Phenotype: The phenotype refers to the observable characteristics or traits of an organism, which are the result of both genetic and environmental influences. It is not related to the position of a gene on a chromosome.
b) Junction: "Junction" typically refers to a point where two things meet or join. In the context of genetics, it does not specifically describe the position of a gene on a chromosome.

c) Locus: This is the correct option. The locus is the specific position or location of a gene on a chromosome. It identifies the site where a particular gene is situated within the DNA sequence of a chromosome.

d) Genotype: The genotype refers to the genetic makeup of an organism, including the specific combination of alleles (gene variants) that an individual possesses. While genotype is related to the genes themselves, it does not specifically refer to the position of a gene on a chromosome.

questions.explanation: A locus is the specific physical location of a gene or other DNA sequence on a chromosome, like a genetic street address.

questions.text: If all the members of a population are homozygous for the same allele, that allele is said to be:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is not correct. If all individuals in a population have the same allele, then that allele is not random in the population's gene pool; rather it is fixed.
This concept of an allele being random in a species does not make sense because alleles are specific variations of genes that exist within a population, not across an entire species
If all the members of a population are homozygous for the same allele, that allele is said to be fixed in the gene pool, not fixed in the population pool.

If all individuals in a population are homozygous for the same allele, then that allele is fixed in the population's gene pool, meaning that it is the only allele present for that particular trait in the population.

questions.explanation: Explanation:If all individuals in a population are homozygous for the same allele, then that allele is fixed in the population's gene pool, meaning that it is the only allele present for that particular trait in the population.This option is not correct. If all individuals in a population have the same allele, then that allele is not random in the population's gene pool; rather it is fixed.This concept of an allele being random in a species does not make sense because alleles are specific variations of genes that exist within a population, not across an entire species.If all the members of a population are homozygous for the same allele, that allele is said to be fixed in the gene pool, not fixed in the population pool.

questions.text: The karyotype of an individual is _ of chromosomes.

options.explanation: Does not refer only on number of chromosome
Does not refer to the type of chromosomes only
Does not refer to chemical composition of chromosomes
Karyotype of an individual refers to complete set of chromosomes that includes the number and set of chromosomes in that set

questions.text: It is the basic unit of biological information:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A trait, as related to genetics, is a specific characteristic of an individual. Traits can be determined by genes, environmental factors or by a combination of both. Traits can be qualitative (such as eye color) or quantitative (such as height or blood pressure).

Genes are made up of DNA. Some genes act as instructions to make molecules called proteins. However, many genes do not code for proteins. In humans, genes vary in size from a few hundred DNA bases to more than 2 million bases.

In a broad sense, the term "genotype" refers to the genetic makeup of an organism; in other words, it describes an organism's complete set of genes. In a more narrow sense, the term can be used to refer to the alleles, or variant forms of a gene, that are carried by an organism.
The observable characteristics in an individual resulting from the expression of genes; the clinical presentation of an individual with a particular genotype.

questions.explanation: A gene is the basic physical and functional unit of heredity. Genes are made up of DNA. Some genes act as instructions to make molecules called proteins. However, many genes do not code for proteins.

questions.text: According to the Hardy-Weinberg theorem, frequencies of alleles and genotypes in a population's gene pool remain:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Dynamic unless acted upon by agents other than recombination
static unless acted upon by agents other than recombination
Constant in the absence of other evolutionary influences

questions.explanation: According to Hardy-Weinberg principle, "The frequencies of alleles and genotypes in a population’s gene pool remain constant over the generations unless acted upon by agents other than sexual recombination." (PTB)

questions.text: When a gene expresses the effects of a gene at another focus, this is known as
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Epistasis is a phenomenon in genetics in which the effect of a gene mutation is dependent on the presence or absence of mutations in one or more other genes. It is an example of non allelic interaction
In Co Dominance, both the contrasting alleles at the same locus express independently without influencing each other so the phenotype of both the alleled become apparent.
The phenomenon whereby, in an individual containing two allelic forms of a gene, one is expressed to the exclusion of the other is known as complete Dominance.

questions.explanation: Epistasis is a phenomenon in genetics in which the effect of a gene mutation is dependent on the presence or absence of mutations in one or more other genes. It is an example of non allelic interaction

Y chromosome has an SRY gene. It is the sex determining region Y gene. It is Y linked gene because it is found only on the Y chromosome.

questions.text: A gene which affects two or more unrelated characteristics is called
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Pleiotropy is defined as the expression of multiple traits by a single gene. A simple example of Pleiotropy is phenylketonuria which is a disease. It is a genetic disorder caused by the low metabolism of the amino acid phenylalanine in the body cells.

Epistasis is a condition where the expression of one gene is modified (e.g., masked, inhibited, or suppressed) by the expression of one or more other genes. The gene which suppresses the effect of other genes is called the Epistatic gene and the gene which is suppressed is known as the hypostatic gene.
A mutant gene is a gene that has undergone a permanent change in the DNA sequence that makes up a gene

Pleiotropy is defined as the expression of multiple traits by a single gene. A simple example of Pleiotropy is phenylketonuria which is a disease. It is a genetic disorder caused by the low metabolism of the amino acid phenylalanine in the body cells.

questions.text: Sickle cell anemia is a type of
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Insertion is a type of mutation that involves the addition of one or more nucleotides into a segment of DNA. An insertion can involve the addition of any number of nucleotides, from a single nucleotide to an entire piece of a chromosome.
DNA transposition is the movement of a defined DNA segment (a transposon) from one genomic site to another.

A deletion is a type of mutation that involves the loss of one or more nucleotides from a segment of DNA. A deletion can involve the loss of any number of nucleotides, from a single nucleotide to an entire piece of a chromosome.

questions.text: Which of the following shows significant effect of Genetic drift ?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Genetic drift: it is the change in frequency of alleles at a locus that occurs by chance.Option A contradicts this fact!
Genetic drift: it is the change in frequency of alleles at a locus that occurs by chance.Option B contradicts this fact!
Genetic drift: it is the change in frequency of alleles at a locus that occurs by chance.Option C contradicts this fact!

•Genetic drift is more likely to occur in small populations because in small populations allele frequencies are likely to change rapidly and dramatically over very few generations or drift because of random events. This rapid change can occur in small populations because each individual.s alleles represent a large fraction of the gene pool. If an individual did not reproduce it could have a much larger effect than in the case of an individual in a large population not reproducing.•In small populations such fluctuations may lead to the loss of particular alleles. This may occur in small populations when a few individuals fail to reproduce and then genes are lost from the population.

questions.explanation: Genetic drift: it is the change in frequency of alleles at a locus that occurs by chance.Option A B C contradicts this fact!Why genetic drift occurs in a small population?•Genetic drift is more likely to occur in small populations because in small populations allele frequencies are likely to change rapidly and dramatically over very few generations or drift because of random events. This rapid change can occur in small populations because each individual.s alleles represent a large fraction of the gene pool. If an individual did not reproduce it could have a much larger effect than in the case of an individual in a large population not reproducing.•In small populations such fluctuations may lead to the loss of particular alleles. This may occur in small populations when a few individuals fail to reproduce and then genes are lost from the population.

The genotype of an organism is its complete set of genetic material.
A trait, as related to genetics, is a specific characteristic of an individual.
An organism, gene, or chromosome that is different from the wild type by one or more (new) characteristics as caused by mutation is termed as mutant and such a trait is called mutant trait.

questions.explanation: The observable characteristics of an organism are termed as its phenotype. The term covers the organism's morphology, its developmental processes, its biochemical and physiological properties, its behavior, and the products of behavior.

questions.text: Black coat color in horses is caused by a dominant allele, while white coat color is due to the recessive allele. Two black horses produce a foal with a coat, if they were to produce a second foal, what would be the probability of the second foal having a black coat?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This would be the probability if both parents were homozygous recessive, which they are not.
This would be the probability of producing a foal with a white coat, not a black coat.
This would be correct if one parent was homozygous dominant and the other was homozygous recessive, which is not the case here.
This is the correct probability since both parents are likely carriers of one recessive allele (heterozygous), leading to a 3:1 ratio in the offspring.
This would be the probability if both parents were homozygous dominant, which cannot be determined from the information given.

questions.explanation: If the black coat color in horses is the dominant phenotype, and both parental horses are black, then they must have at least one allele for the black coat color. Because the white coat color is the recessive phenotype, it can be caused only by a homozygous recessive genotype. The probability of these horses producing a foal with a black coat is 75%, or 3 out of 4.

questions.text: The proportion of different alleles of a gene in a population is termed as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Allele frequency refers to the proportion of a specific allele within a population, calculated by considering the total number of alleles contributed by individuals with different genotypes at a particular locus
Genetic drift is the change in frequency of an existing gene variant in the population due to random chance.
The proportion of different allele of a gene in population is termed as gene frequency.
Allelomorph is another term used for allele. It is a synonym of allele

questions.explanation: Allele frequency refers to the proportion of a specific allele within a population, calculated by considering the total number of alleles contributed by individuals with different genotypes at a particular locus

questions.text: In a population, 36% of people have "Blue Eyes."Allele frequency for Blue Eyes is?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect. Phenotype frequency does not equal allele frequency. Blue eyes are a result of a recessive allele, and the allele frequency should be calculated using the Hardy-Weinberg principle, considering homozygous recessive individuals.
Incorrect. This option does not accurately reflect the calculation based on homozygous recessive genotype.
Incorrect. This option overestimates the allele frequency, assuming more heterozygous individuals than possible.
Correct. Using the Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, the square root of the population with the recessive phenotype (36%) gives the allele frequency, which is 6%.

questions.explanation: To determine the allele frequency of a recessive trait like blue eyes, we use the Hardy-Weinberg principle. Given that 36% of the population has blue eyes, these individuals are homozygous recessive (bb). The allele frequency for b is the square root of 0.36, which is 0.6 or 60%. However, to find the frequency of the allele itself, we need to divide it by 2, resulting in 6%.Options A, B, and C are incorrect because they do not correctly apply the Hardy-Weinberg calculations for determining allele frequencies from phenotype frequencies.

questions.text: The terminal ends of chromosomes are called _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Satellite or satellite DNA refers to a class of highly repetitive DNA sequences that are often found in tandem repeats near the centromeres of chromosomes. As it does not match the given statement, this option is incorrect.
Kinetochores are protein structures located on centrosomes of chromosomes. They are not located on the terminal ends of chromosomes, so this option is incorrect.
Nuclear organization refers to the arrangement or organization of DNA within the nucleus. It does not relate to the given statement, so the option is incorrect.
Telomeres are repetitive sequences of DNA that are located at the ends of linear chromosomes in eukaryotic cells. As it perfectly fits the given statement, this option is the correct option.

questions.explanation: The terminal ends of a chromosome are called telomeres. Hence, option D is the correct option.

2pq represents heterozygous.
Heterozygote shows dominant phenotype.
q2 represents homozygous recessive.
The basic unit of heredity that is passed on from parent to child.

questions.explanation: In the Hardy-Weinberg principle, p2 is homozygous dominant, 2pq is heterozygous and q2 is homozygous recessive.

questions.text: Dominance is the physiological effect of an allele over its partner allele occupying the;
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is not correct. Alleles that occupy the same locus on the same chromosome are called alleles or genes in a cis-configuration.
This option is also incorrect. Alleles that occupy different loci on the same chromosome are called alleles or genes in a trans-configuration.
Dominance is the physiological effect of an allele over its partner allele occuping the same locus on respective homologue.
This option is also incorrect. Alleles that occupy different loci on their respective homologous chromosomes are called non-allelic genes.

questions.explanation: Dominance is the physiological effect of an allele over its partner allele occuping the same locus on respective homologue. Therefore, option C is correct.

questions.text: The term Gene was coined by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Wilhelm Johannsen was a Danish botanist who coined the term "gene" in 1909 to describe the fundamental physical and functional unit of heredity. He distinguished between the genotype (the genetic makeup of an organism) and the phenotype (the observable characteristics).

Correns: Carl Correns was one of the scientists who independently rediscovered Gregor Mendel's work on the principles of heredity around 1900. While crucial to the understanding of genes, he did not coin the term.

Tscharmach: Erich von Tschermak was another scientist who independently rediscovered Mendel's work at the same time as Correns and Hugo de Vries. Like Correns, he played a key role in bringing Mendel's laws to light but did not coin the term "gene."

Purkinje: Jan Evangelista Purkyně was a Czech physiologist and anatomist known for his extensive research on cells, tissues, and various organs. His work predates the understanding of genes as units of heredity.

questions.explanation: The term "Gene" was coined by the Danish botanist Wilhelm Johannsen. He introduced the term 'gene' in the early 20th century to describe the basic unit of heredity responsible for the transmission of traits from parents to offspring.

questions.text: If red and white color flowers in mirabulus jalapa are crossed, the F1 generation will show:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: If the red and white color alleles were both completely dominant or exhibited complete dominance, then the F1 generation would indeed display all red-colored flowers. However, this is not the case in Mirabilis jalapa.

Similarly, if the white color allele were completely dominant over the red color allele, then the F1 generation would show all white-colored flowers. However, this is not observed in Mirabilis jalapa.

While it is true that the F1 generation will exhibit a pink phenotype, it is not the case that all flowers will be uniformly pink. The pink color arises due to the blending of the red and white colors, resulting in a range of shades of pink.

When red and white color flowers in Mirabilis jalapa (Four O'Clock Flower) are crossed, the F1 generation will typically display an intermediate phenotype, resulting in pink-colored flowers. This is due to incomplete dominance or blending inheritance, where neither the red nor white color allele is completely dominant over the other. In this case, the red color is typically controlled by the presence of a dominant allele (R), while the white color is controlled by a recessive allele (r). When the red and white flowers are crossed, the F1 generation will inherit one copy of the dominant red allele (R) from one parent and one copy of the recessive white allele (r) from the other parent. As a result, the F1 plants will have a heterozygous genotype (Rr) and exhibit an intermediate phenotype, which is pink-colored flowers.

questions.explanation: When red and white color flowers in Mirabilis jalapa (Four O'Clock Flower) are crossed, the F1 generation will typically display an intermediate phenotype, resulting in pink-colored flowers. This is due to incomplete dominance or blending inheritance, where neither the red nor white color allele is completely dominant over the other. In this case, the red color is typically controlled by the presence of a dominant allele (R), while the white color is controlled by a recessive allele (r). When the red and white flowers are crossed, the F1 generation will inherit one copy of the dominant red allele (R) from one parent and one copy of the recessive white allele (r) from the other parent. As a result, the F1 plants will have a heterozygous genotype (Rr) and exhibit an intermediate phenotype, which is pink-colored flowers.

questions.text: Which of one of the following characteristics in man is controlled by a recessive gone?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The tongue rolling ability occurs due to the influence of a dominant allele of the gene. A person who has either one or two copies of the dominant allele will be able to twist their tongue. In the case that a person is born with two recessive alleles, they cannot twist their tongue. Rolling the tongue into a tube shape is often described as a dominant trait with simple Mendelian inheritance, and it is commonly referenced in introductory and genetic biology courses. The inability to roll the tongue is a recessive trait.

Diabetes is defined as a condition in which the blood glucose level rises above the normal level which is due to less production of insulin whose function is to convert glucose sugars into non-glucose sugars by storing it in the form of glucagon. Diabetes has two types Type I and Type II. 90 percent of patients with Type II diabetes have no relation with genetic factors while 40 percent of diabetes type I patients have some relation with genetic factors. Most of them are autosomal dominant.

Skin colour is a polygenic trait which is controlled by many alleles meaning it is a continuously varying trait and their intensity depends upon the number of effect causing alleles. Skin colour is controlled by three gene pairs. Each pair has two alleles in which one allele is dominant and other allele is recessive. The dominant alleles are known as dark-skin allele that code for melanin production and the recesiive alleles are light skin alleles that inhibit melanin production. Neither allele is completely dominant on the other allele. Therefore, the heterozygous exhibits intermediate phenotype (incomplete dominance).

Eye color is determined by variations in a person's genes. Most of the genes associated with eye color are involved in the production, transport, or storage of a pigment called melanin. The trait that is hidden is called recessive. Brown eye color is a dominant trait and blue eye color is a recessive trait. Green eye color is a mix of both. Green is recessive to brown but dominant to blue.

questions.explanation: This mcq can only be solved by elimination trick. Tongue rolling is controlled by dominant trait while skin colour is a polygenic trait and exhibits intermediate phenotype due to co-dominance. This rules out option A and D. Diabetes is not usually linked with genetic factors and if it is even linked it is only autosomal dominant. This makes eye colour the best option in which blue eye colour is controlled by recessive gene.

questions.text: In which case the genotypic and.phenolypic ratio·will be 1:2:1?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In complete dominance, one allele completely masks the effect of another, resulting in a phenotypic ratio of 3:1 and a genotypic ratio of 1:2:1 for heterozygous crosses, but not both being 1:2:1.
In incomplete dominance, the heterozygous phenotype is an intermediate blend of the two homozygous phenotypes, leading to a genotypic and phenotypic ratio of 1:2:1.
In co-dominance, both alleles express themselves fully in the phenotype, resulting in a distinct phenotype for each allele but the ratios do not yield 1:2:1.
This option suggests that none of the conditions apply, but incomplete dominance specifically results in a 1:2:1 ratio, making this option incorrect.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option B: Incomplete dominance. In cases of incomplete dominance, the heterozygous offspring exhibit a phenotype that is a blend of the two homozygous phenotypes, leading to a genotypic ratio of 1:2:1 and a corresponding phenotypic ratio of 1:2:1. This is distinct from complete dominance, where one allele masks the other (Option A), resulting in a 3:1 phenotypic ratio. Co-dominance (Option C) allows both alleles to be fully expressed, but does not yield a 1:2:1 ratio. Lastly, Option D is incorrect because incomplete dominance does indeed fulfill the condition specified in the question.

questions.text: The Hardy-Weinberg principle applies to.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The Hardy-Weinberg principle, also known as the Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, is a fundamental concept in population genetics. It describes the relationship between the frequencies of alleles and genotypes in a population that is not evolving.According to the Hardy-Weinberg principle, in a large, randomly mating population with no mutation, migration, genetic drift, or natural selection, the frequencies of alleles and genotypes in the population will remain constant from generation to generation. The principle provides a mathematical framework to understand the inheritance of genetic traits within a population over time.The other options you provided (A) the cell theory, (B) the theory of spontaneous generation, and (E) the germ theory of disease, are unrelated to the Hardy-Weinberg principle. (C) experimental embryology is a broad field of study that includes various experimental approaches to understanding embryonic development, but it is not directly related to the Hardy-Weinberg principle, which specifically deals with population genetics.

questions.text: A pea plant with yellow seeds was crossed to a plant having green seeds. What will happen in the F1 generation; if the plants are true breeding?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Half of the seeds will be yellow
options.explanation: suggests that half of the seeds in the F1 generation will be yellow. This statement is not correct. In genetics, the color of the seeds is determined by the expression of specific alleles at the seed color gene locus. If one parent has yellow seeds and the other has green seeds, and both parents are true-breeding (homozygous for their respective alleles), then all the F1 generation seeds will have the same genotype, which will be heterozygous for the seed color gene. In this case, the dominant yellow allele (Y) will mask the recessive green allele (y), resulting in all the seeds in the F1 generation being yellow.

options.text: All of the the seeds will be green
options.explanation: suggests that all of the seeds in the F1 generation will be green. This statement is not correct. As mentioned above, the dominant yellow allele (Y) will mask the recessive green allele (y) in the F1 generation, resulting in all the seeds being yellow.

options.text: Both phenotypes will be present in a ratio of 1:2:1
options.explanation: Suggests that both phenotypes (yellow and green seeds) will be present in a ratio of 1:2:1 in the F1 generation. This statement is not correct. The correct phenotypic ratio in the F1 generation of a cross between a true-breeding yellow-seeded plant (YY) and a true-breeding green-seeded plant (yy) is 100% yellow seeds (Yy).

options.text: All the seeds will be yellow
options.explanation: suggests that all the seeds in the F1 generation will be yellow. This statement is correct. Since the yellow seed color is dominant (Y) over the green seed color (y), all the seeds in the F1 generation will have the dominant allele (Y) and, therefore, be yellow.

questions.explanation: Option (d) suggests that all the seeds in the F1 generation will be yellow. This statement is correct. Since yellow seed color is dominant (Y) over green seed color (y), all the seeds in the F1 generation will have the dominant allele (Y) and, therefore, be yellow.

questions.text: A certain type of plant is only tall when it has a heterozygous genotype. If two heterozygous plants are crossed, what is the probability their offspring will also be tall?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: If a certain type of plant is only tall when it has a heterozygous genotype, it suggests that the tall trait is dominant and the short trait is recessive. Let's use "T" to represent the dominant allele for tallness and "t" to represent the recessive allele for shortness. When two heterozygous plants (Tt x Tt) are crossed, each parent contributes one allele for tallness (T) and one allele for shortness (t). The possible genotypes of the offspring are: TT (tall), Tt (tall), Tt (tall, tt (short) Out of these four possible genotypes, three of them result in the tall phenotype (TT, Tt, Tt). Therefore, the probability that the offspring will be tall is 3 out of 4 possible outcomes. However, since this plant can only be tall if heterozygous, then 2/4 i.e., 50% of the offsprings will be tall.

questions.text: Your neighbour has a flower garden in which there are red flowers and white flowers. These flowers are diploid organisms, and the flower colour is an autosomal trait. questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: If there is only the Rr allele, the flower will be red instead of white.
If the alleles are Rr and RR, both will produce red flowers. In case, if no 'R' is present and 'rr' are present, the flower will be white instead of red.
If alleles are 'rr' the flower will be white instead of red.

A red flower must have an 'R' allele which conveys the red phenotype. Since the red allele is dominant, the flower remains as long as red as one of the copies of this allele is present. Thus, both the 'Rr' and 'RR' will produce red flowers. In case, if no 'R' allele is present and 'rr' alleles are present, the flower will be white instead of red.

questions.text: What is the phenotypic ratio for a cross between a plant with blue flowers (BB) and a plant with white flowers (bb)?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: 25% blue, 75% white: This ratio is incorrect because the plant with blue flowers has a genotype of BB, meaning it carries two dominant alleles for blue flower color. When crossed with a plant with white flowers (bb), all the offspring will inherit one dominant allele (B) from the blue-flowered parent and one recessive allele (b) from the white-flowered parent. As a result, all The offspring will have a genotype of Bb, which expresses the dominant blue flower color. Therefore, the phenotypic ratio will be 100% blue, not 25% blue.

75% blue, 25% white: This ratio is incorrect for the same reason as option A. blue-flowered parent (BB) will contribute a dominant allele (B) to all the offspring, resulting in a 100% phenotypic ratio of blue flowers

All white: This ratio is incorrect because the blue-flowered parent (BB) carries two dominant alleles for blue flower color. When crossed with the white-flowered parent (bb), all the offspring will inherit one dominant allele (B) from the blue-flowered parent and one recessive allele (b) from the white-flowered parent. The presence of the dominant allele will result in the expression of the blue flower color, so all the offspring will have blue flowers, not white.

The phenotypic ratio for the cross between a plant with blue flowers (BB) and a plant with white flowers (BB) is 100% blue. The correct answer is "All blue." Let's understand why. When it comes to flower color in this specific cross, blue (B) is dominant over white (b). The plant with blue flowers is homozygous dominant (BB), meaning it has two copies of the blue allele. The plant with white flowers is homozygous recessive (bb), meaning it has two copies of the white allele. In genetics, when a dominant allele is present, it masks the expression of the recessive allele. Since the blue allele is dominant, any plant that inherits at least one copy of the blue allele will have blue flowers. In this cross, all offspring will inherit one copy of the blue allele from the parent with blue flowers and one copy of the white allele from the parent with white flowers. The resulting genotype for all the offspring will be Bb, which corresponds to the blue flower phenotype.

questions.explanation: The phenotypic ratio for a cross between a plant with blue flowers (BB) and a plant with white flowers (bb) would be 100% blue. In this case, the parent plants are homozygous for their respective flower colors, meaning they have two identical alleles for flower color (BB and bb). When they are crossed, all of their offspring will inherit one allele from each parent, resulting in all the offspring having the genotype Bb. This genotype represents the heterozygous condition for flower color, which expresses the dominant blue flower color phenotype.
